title: Staining 3D Broccoli-Based Scaffolds for Microstructure Visualization
author: Catherine A. Sword

steps.title: Transfer desired number of scaffolds to...
steps.instruction: Transfer desired number of scaffolds to a cover-glass-bottomed dish or chamber (e.g. Ibidi 80807). Plastic dishes such as well plates may be used but produce higher background fluorescence and are generally not compatible with microscope objectives > 10x.

steps.title: Pipette 100 µl each of Calcofluor...
steps.instruction: Pipette 100 µl each of Calcofluor White Honeywell Fluka Catalog #18909 and 10% w/v 10 Mass / % volume potassium hydroxide Acros Organics Catalog #42414 onto each scaffold.

steps.title: Incubate at Room temperature for at...
steps.instruction: Incubate at Room temperature for at least 00:10:00 to allow the stain to penetrate and bind to the scaffold.

steps.title: Rinse the scaffolds once with deionized water
steps.instruction: Rinse the scaffolds once with deionized water.

steps.title: Add enough deionized water to the...
steps.instruction: Add enough deionized water to the scaffolds to keep them hydrated, but not so much that the scaffolds float above the dish/well surface. Having the scaffolds stationary on the surface will make them easier to image.

steps.title: Transfer the dish/chamber/plate to an inverted...
steps.instruction: Transfer the dish/chamber/plate to an inverted fluorescence microscope and examine using the same settings used to examine DAPI. For example, on a widefield microscope use 392/22 nm excitation and 447/60 emission filters, and on a laser scanning confocal microscope excite with a 405 nm laser and collect emission in the range 425-441 nm. These ranges are just examples and other similar settings will also work.
